questions.prompt: Explain why TypeScript being structurally typed means you cannot rely on the type system alone to stop a UserId string from being passed where an OrderId string is expected.
successCriteria: Identifies that identical shapes (both plain strings) are interchangeable under structural typing
Notes the type system won’t object
Optionally forward-references branding

rubric.excellent: Clearly explains structural interchangeability of same-shaped primitives and that only branding (s3) recovers the distinction.
rubric.adequate: States both strings are structurally identical so the compiler allows the swap.
rubric.weak: Misattributes the cause or claims the compiler would catch it.

errorPatterns.description: Asserts the compiler would prevent the swap, missing structural interchangeability.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: resurface retrieval rp-s1-contrast-idtypes and worked-example we-s1-structural

questions.prompt: A variable has type A | B | C. After two guard clauses eliminate A and B, what is the variable’s type in the remaining code, and what is this mechanism called?
successCriteria: States the type is C
Names control-flow narrowing (by elimination/exhaustion)

rubric.excellent: Answers C and names control-flow narrowing/narrowing by elimination precisely.
rubric.adequate: Answers C and refers to narrowing.
rubric.weak: Gives a wrong remaining type or cannot name the mechanism.

errorPatterns.description: Believes the variable remains A | B | C or names the wrong mechanism.

assessments.title: Generics and Type-Level Programming Mastery Check

questions.prompt: Key difference between function id<T>(x: T): T and function id(x: any): any? (A) None (B) The generic preserves the caller’s specific type; any discards it (C) The any version is type-safe, the generic isn’t (D) Only the generic compiles
successCriteria: Selects B

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains relationship preservation.
rubric.weak: Selects A or C, equating generics with any.

errorPatterns.description: Treats a generic as equivalent to any, missing preserved input/output relationship.

questions.prompt: type R = ElementOf<string[]> where type ElementOf<T> = T extends (infer E)[] ? E : never. R is? (A) string[] (B) never (C) string (D) any
successCriteria: Selects C
Explains infer binds the element type

rubric.excellent: Selects C and explains infer E captures the element type.
rubric.weak: Selects B or A, misreading infer.

errorPatterns.description: Misinterprets what infer captures from the array pattern.

questions.prompt: type X = ToArray<'a' | 'b'> with type ToArray<T> = T extends any ? T[] : never. X is? (A) ('a' | 'b')[] (B) 'a'[] | 'b'[] (C) string[] (D) never
successCriteria: Selects B
Recognizes distribution over the naked parameter

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains per-member distribution plus the tuple-wrap suppression.
rubric.weak: Selects A, collapsing the conditional over the whole union.

errorPatterns.description: Answers ('a' | 'b')[], treating the distributive conditional as a single if over the union.

questions.prompt: Explain in one or two sentences why over-parameterizing a generic type can hurt usability, and what to reach for instead.
successCriteria: Notes uninferable parameters become caller burden / hurt readability
Recommends constraints and defaults

rubric.excellent: Explains the caller-burden/readability cost and prescribes constraints + defaults.
rubric.adequate: Mentions one of cost or remedy.
rubric.weak: Claims more parameters are always better.

errorPatterns.description: Argues more generic parameters always means more flexibility.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: flag for instructor review and resurface b-s2-main over-parameterizing passage

questions.prompt: A colleague claims keyof Config will let them loop over the keys at runtime. Correct them: what does keyof actually produce, and what runtime construct do they need?
successCriteria: States keyof yields a compile-time union of key literals (a type), erased at runtime
Names Object.keys() for runtime iteration

rubric.excellent: Distinguishes the type-level union from a runtime array and prescribes Object.keys().
rubric.adequate: States keyof is a type and names Object.keys().
rubric.weak: Treats keyof as runtime reflection.

errorPatterns.description: Believes keyof/typeof/conditional types perform runtime reflection.

questions.prompt: Why is a discriminated union preferable to an object of optional fields for request state? (A) Less runtime memory (B) Permits only one valid variant at a time, eliminating contradictory combinations (C) Optional fields don’t compile (D) It avoids generics
successCriteria: Selects B

rubric.excellent: Selects B and gives the loading+data+error example.
rubric.weak: Selects A, C or D.

errorPatterns.description: Believes optional fields adequately model exclusive states.

questions.prompt: In an exhaustiveness check, what makes the compiler error when a new union variant is added? (A) New variant is any (B) The unhandled variant is no longer narrowed to never, so the never assignment fails (C) switch statements can’t grow (D) Literal types are runtime values
successCriteria: Selects B

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains the default-branch narrowing.
rubric.weak: Selects another option.

errorPatterns.description: Cannot explain why the never assignment fails for an added variant.

questions.prompt: Branded types defeat which TypeScript behaviour to prevent mixing UserId and OrderId? (A) Type erasure (B) Control-flow narrowing (C) Structural typing (D) Generic inference
successCriteria: Selects C

rubric.excellent: Selects C and explains the phantom marker making shapes differ.
rubric.weak: Selects another option.

errorPatterns.description: Dismisses branding or cannot identify structural typing as the defeated behaviour.

questions.prompt: Explain why the __brand phantom property does not need to exist at runtime, referencing what you learned in s1 about type erasure.
successCriteria: States types (including the phantom field) are erased at compile time
Notes branding works purely at compile time for assignability

rubric.excellent: Connects erasure (s1) to the phantom field’s compile-time-only role.
rubric.adequate: States the field is erased and only matters to the compiler.
rubric.weak: Thinks __brand exists at runtime.

errorPatterns.description: Believes the brand field exists at runtime.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: resurface b-s1-main erasure passage and worked-example we-s3-brand

questions.prompt: Choosing between (a) Order with status: string plus optional fields and (b) a discriminated union of order states, give two concrete illegal states model (a) permits that model (b) forbids.
successCriteria: Names two concrete contradictory states (e.g. shipped+cancelled, paid status with no payment fields, two statuses at once)
Shows (b) makes each unconstructable

rubric.excellent: Gives two precise illegal combinations and explains why the union forbids each.
rubric.adequate: Gives at least one valid illegal state forbidden by the union.
rubric.weak: Cannot produce a concrete illegal state.

errorPatterns.description: Fails to identify any illegal state the optional-field model permits.

questions.prompt: Why is const u = (await res.json()) as User unsafe? (A) as is slower than satisfies (B) The assertion is compile-time only; no runtime check confirms the data is actually a User (C) res.json() returns a string (D) User can’t be asserted
successCriteria: Selects B

rubric.excellent: Selects B and notes the payload could be malformed with no check.
rubric.weak: Selects another option.

errorPatterns.description: Believes the annotation/assertion makes fetched data safe.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: resurface interaction i-s4-typed-fetch and worked-example we-s4-guard

questions.prompt: A function isUser(x: unknown): x is User. After if (isUser(x)), inside the block x is: (A) Still unknown (B) any (C) Narrowed to User (D) never
successCriteria: Selects C
Notes narrowing depends on real checks

rubric.excellent: Selects C and notes the guard must perform real checks.
rubric.weak: Selects another option.

errorPatterns.description: Does not recognize a type predicate narrows the argument.

questions.prompt: The main advantage of deriving a type with z.infer from a schema is: (A) Faster runtime validation (B) The validator and the static type share one source and can’t drift (C) Removes the need for any runtime checks (D) Smaller bundle size
successCriteria: Selects B

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains drift prevention.
rubric.weak: Selects C, thinking derivation removes runtime checks.

errorPatterns.description: Believes schema-derived typing or existing types remove the need for runtime validation.

questions.prompt: Connect this section to s1: using the fact that types are erased, explain why runtime validation is required and not merely good practice at API boundaries.
successCriteria: States types are gone at runtime so nothing in the program enforces them on external data
Concludes only running validation code can guarantee shape

rubric.excellent: Tightly links erasure to the necessity of runtime checks at boundaries.
rubric.adequate: States erasure means the type can’t enforce the shape at runtime.
rubric.weak: Treats validation as optional/stylistic.

errorPatterns.description: Frames runtime validation as merely good practice, not a necessity.

questions.prompt: Give the seam-by-seam reasoning for where you’d validate (a) a paginated API list, (b) a multi-field signup form, and (c) environment variables — and which of parse/safeParse fits each.
successCriteria: API: fetch layer once, parse
Form: per-field, safeParse
Config: boot, parse
Justifies by who controls data / what failure means

rubric.excellent: Correct placement and parse/safeParse for all three with sound justification.
rubric.adequate: Correct for at least two seams.
rubric.weak: Mismatches placement or parse/safeParse for most seams.

errorPatterns.description: Chooses the wrong parse/safeParse or validation location for the seams.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: resurface interaction i-s4-seams and worked-example we-s4-config

questions.prompt: In a sound type architecture, the Invoice domain type should be: (A) Hand-written as an interface in a global types.ts (B) Derived from the boundary validation schema, defined once, and reused (C) Re-declared in each component that needs it (D) Typed as any for flexibility
successCriteria: Selects B

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains the provenance chain.
rubric.weak: Selects A, C or D.

errorPatterns.description: Prefers hand-written/re-declared domain types over schema-derived single source of truth.

questions.prompt: What does const x = {...} satisfies SomeType do that const x: SomeType = {...} does not? (A) Performs runtime validation (B) Checks conformance while preserving x’s narrow/literal inferred type (C) Erases the type at compile time (D) Allows extra properties
successCriteria: Selects B

rubric.excellent: Selects B and contrasts with annotation widening and as not checking.
rubric.weak: Selects another option.

errorPatterns.description: Misunderstands satisfies as runtime validation or as equivalent to an annotation.

questions.prompt: The best framing of enabling noUncheckedIndexedAccess is: (A) Adds annoying errors with no benefit (B) Relocates a class of ‘missing value’ crashes from runtime to compile time (C) Speeds up the runtime (D) Only matters for arrays, not records
successCriteria: Selects B

rubric.excellent: Selects B and frames each error as a pre-empted incident.
rubric.weak: Selects A, misframing strictness as noise.

errorPatterns.description: Frames strictness flags as annoyance with no benefit.

questions.prompt: Explain, citing s3 and s4, how a single schema can be the origin of both runtime-validated data and the compile-time Resource<T> state used by a component — and why that prevents drift.
successCriteria: Schema validates at runtime (s4) and derives the domain type (s3)
Domain type feeds the generic Resource<T> state and props
One origin means a change ripples everywhere, preventing drift

rubric.excellent: Traces schema -> validated data + derived type -> state -> props and explains drift prevention via single origin.
rubric.adequate: Connects schema to both validation and type with a drift argument.
rubric.weak: Misses the single-origin/drift link.

errorPatterns.description: Cannot connect one schema to both runtime data and compile-time state, missing drift prevention.

questions.prompt: A teammate proposes ‘ship features now, add the type infrastructure next quarter.’ Give the architectural argument against this, naming the specific failures (duplication, any leakage) that result from retrofitting.
successCriteria: Notes uncoordinated layers cause re-declared shapes and any in the gaps
Concludes retrofitting becomes a rewrite
Frames the plan-now vs rewrite-later asymmetry

rubric.excellent: Names duplication and any leakage, explains the rewrite cost, and states the asymmetry argument.
rubric.adequate: Names at least one failure and the rewrite risk.
rubric.weak: Accepts retrofitting as low-cost.

errorPatterns.description: Believes type infrastructure can be added late with no architectural cost.

glossary.definition: Compatibility determined by a type’s members (shape), not its declared name.
Compatibility determined by explicit declared relationships (e.g. implements/extends); TypeScript is not nominal by default.
Whether a value of one type may be placed where another type is expected; a source is assignable if it satisfies every requirement of the target.
An opt-out of type checking; assignable to and from everything and propagating, so it silently disables safety.
The top type that accepts any value but requires narrowing before use; the type-safe counterpart to any.
The bottom type with no possible values; signals unreachable or impossible states.
A compile-time-only instruction to treat a value as a given type; it has no runtime effect, conversion, or check.
The compiler’s tracking of how a variable’s type changes across branches.
Refining a broad type to a more specific one within a code path.
An expression or function that narrows a type (typeof, instanceof, in, equality, or custom predicate functions).
A placeholder type filled per use that relates input and output types, preserving type information.
A bound limiting what a type parameter may be, unlocking its members.
A fallback type used when none is supplied or inferred.
The place the compiler reads to deduce a type parameter.
The type of a property at key K.
A type that iterates over a type’s keys to build a new type.
Renaming or filtering keys inside a mapped type (mapping to never drops a key).
A string-literal type built by interpolating other types.
A type of the form T extends U ? X : Y that selects a branch by a type relationship.
A keyword that captures (binds) a type from within a conditional type’s pattern.
A conditional over a naked union type parameter that applies per member then unions the results.
Designing types so that illegal combinations cannot be constructed.
A union whose members share a literal discriminant property used by the compiler to narrow to one variant.
The shared literal property (e.g. status) the compiler switches on to narrow a union.
Using a never assignment in the default branch to force handling of every union variant.
A primitive intersected with a unique phantom marker to make structurally-identical types distinct.
A type-only field with no runtime presence, used for branding.
Type-level immutability that prevents mutation through a reference.
An assertion giving a value its narrowest, deeply-readonly literal type.
Any point where external, untyped data enters the system.
Executing real checks against data’s actual shape at run time.
A type-guard return type whose truth narrows its argument to T.
A runtime description of valid data from which both validation and a static type are derived.
A static type extracted from a schema (e.g. z.infer).
Throw-on-failure validation (parse) versus return-a-result-union validation (safeParse).
The pattern of validating all external input at the edges so the interior can trust its types.
Each shape defined once and flowing outward, never re-declared.
The typed interface between a component and its callers.
Keeping types beside the code that owns them rather than in a global dumping ground.
Type-only descriptions for untyped JS modules or globals.
Extending existing (often third-party) types in place rather than forking them.
A type-only import the bundler can fully erase.
An operator that validates a value against a type while preserving its narrow inferred type.
Compiler options (strict, strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, etc.) that move defect classes from runtime to compile time.
The lightweight policy coordinating boundary, domain, state, and component types as one system.
